A solo attorney in Chattanooga shouldn't need a $50,000/year software budget to do competent legal research.

But that's the reality right now. The legal technology gap between BigLaw and small-firm practitioners is a justice problem disguised as a software problem.

Here's what I mean: the big firms have AI-powered research tools, automated drafting, and instant conflict checks. They can process in 5 minutes what takes a solo practitioner 2 hours. Same case. Same law. Completely different access to tools.

The good news? That gap is closing fast.

Firms like Husch Blackwell are actively hiring Legal AI Engineers. Companies like Riskonnect and Advanced Solutions International are building AI into enterprise legal and compliance workflows. The legal industry is being rebuilt from the inside out.

What excites me is the downstream effect: when AI handles the research grind, the citation formatting, the conflict checking, and the deadline tracking — attorneys get to do what they actually went to law school for. Advocate. Counsel. Think strategically.

And for the communities that need it most — immigrant families navigating the system, small business owners facing contract disputes, people who can't afford $400/hour — affordable legal AI doesn't just help lawyers. It helps everyone who depends on them.

The playing field is leveling. And it's about time.

Attorneys — what's the one task you wish AI could handle for you tomorrow? Drop it in the comments.

#LegalTech #AIforLawyers #AccessToJustice #LegalInnovation #SmallFirm #FutureOfLaw #Automation

🧭 #AI #Strategy: McKinsey & Company on AI Workforce Transformation!

AI is changing how teams work. The old rules for building a workforce no longer apply.

Organizations must act now to create teams that can win in an agentic era.

Here are the critical moves for designing your technology workforce:

 * Hire Experts, Not Volume: AI is taking over junior-level development tasks. Stop hiring for raw headcount. Instead, focus on senior talent, architects, and designers with deep skills and strong judgment. You need leaders who can orchestrate teams of people and agents.

 * Build Non-Replaceable Skills: Identify the capabilities your organization must own. Invest in deep training for your people on topics like human judgment and platform expertise. Shift your culture to view AI agents as new co-workers who augment your team.

 * Rewrite Vendor Contracts: The old model of paying for time or hours is outdated. Negotiate new deals that link vendor pay directly to business outcomes, such as speed, quality, and specific results.

These moves are not independent. Taken together, this strategy can triple your technology returns and help you generate real ROI.

💾 Save this post to guide your talent strategy, and 📤 share it with your leadership team to start the conversation today.

#McKinseyInsights #DigitalTransformation #TalentStrategy #FutureOfWork

Dear Hiring Managers — domain knowledge is not the filter you think it is.

Every few years, something arrives that no PM was trained for.

Digital Payments — real-time money movement, regulations multiplying across geographies, mandates arriving faster than platforms could absorb. No playbook existed.

E-commerce — conversion funnels, cart abandonment, personalisation at scale. No university course covered that.

APIs and microservices — the monolith died and you had to think in contracts and distributed systems. No certification for that either.

Mobile-first — a 5-inch screen rewrote every flow, notification, and user journey. From scratch.

Scaled Agile — SAFe, LeSS, PI Planning. Moving 10 teams without losing your mind. Or anyone else's.

The product mindset — from "I shipped the roadmap" to "I moved the metric." That rewiring took years.

And now: E-Invoicing — 50+ countries, no unified standard, every government speaking a different regulatory dialect, mandate deadlines shifting without warning, legacy platforms never built for this velocity. A space mid-inflection asking the same question payments asked a decade ago: how do you absorb regulatory change as a capability rather than a crisis?

Alongside it: agentic AI sitting inside enterprise platforms, waiting to be applied.

None of these were domain challenges. They were paradigm shifts.

And every single time — the PMs who thrived leaned in before they were comfortable, built the mental model fast, and brought others along.

That's the actual job at the senior level.

Not mastering one domain. Mastering the adaptation.

When you filter for domain, you're screening for the last war.

Domain knowledge is a starting point. The willingness to move quickly from unfamiliar to fluent is what separates a good PM from a product leader.

The best product leaders weren't afraid of what they didn't know yet.

Hiring managers — do you hire for domain or for the ability to adapt?

Day 1: Mastering Pega Case Management 🚀
Ever wondered how banks handle millions of loan applications without chaos? Here's a real-world Pega Case Management example from Lloyd's Banking Group—they process 40-50 million cases yearly using Pega Cloud! 
Real-Time Scenario: Home Loan Approval Workflow
A customer submits a ₹50 lakh home loan app via mobile banking. Pega creates a Case that auto-routes through Stages:
Submission (2 hrs Goal): Capture docs (Aadhaar, salary slips)—auto-validate uploads.
Document Review (24 hrs Deadline): OCR extracts data; if blurry, escalate to clerk with urgency spike.
Credit Check (4 hrs Goal): Integrate with CIBIL API—score <700? Route to manual underwriter.
Manager Approval (48 hrs Deadline): SLA triggers SMS/email if delayed; urgency hits 100 for prioritization.
Disbursement (1 day): Generate e-contract PDF, push to customer's bank via REST.
Why It Rocks: Cases track end-to-end with stages, steps, SLAs, and escalations—cutting manual tracking by 40% and approval time from weeks to days. Dynamic child cases spawn for KYC if needed! 
Pega's straight-through processing + AI decisioning = compliant, scalable magic for BFSI.
💬 What's your toughest case workflow? Tried Pega for loans? Drop comments! 👇

Good morning all 

One here for the CHRO, CPOs, and HR Directors in Tech.

Is this how you're seeing things?

HR in tech is going through a quiet but fundamental shift, from support function to strategic operator.

For years, HR was built around growth: hiring at scale, building processes, supporting expanding teams.
 
 
That context has changed.

In 2026, the environment looks very different:

Hiring is slower and more selective: Every role needs clear ROI
·       Entry-level pipelines have contracted significantly
·       Recruiting teams have been reduced as AI and efficiency measures scale
·       Internal mobility is now filling a large share of roles
·       Contract and blended workforce models are becoming standard
·       This isn’t just a hiring slowdown. It’s a structural reset.

And it’s redefining what HR is responsible for.

The focus is shifting from:
 → Headcount growth → Workforce optimisation
 → External hiring → Internal redeployment
 → Process delivery → Business impact
 → Job titles → Skills and capabilities

At the centre of this shift is AI.
HR is now expected to:

 • Lead workforce transformation as automation reshapes roles
 • Redesign organisations around skills, not hierarchy
 • Balance cost, productivity, and capability
 • Navigate the ethical and cultural implications of AI adoption

This is a very different mandate.
The HR leaders who are thriving right now are:
 
·       Deeply commercial and data-driven
·       Comfortable with ambiguity and restructuring
·       Focused on skills, not just roles
·       Embedded in business decision-making
 
Meanwhile, traditional HR models built purely around operations and support are under pressure.

The big takeaway:

HR hasn’t become less important, it’s become more accountable.
From shaping org design to guiding AI adoption, HR is now directly tied to how businesses perform.

And that’s a very different seat at the table.

Skills Should Travel Freely — Why Remote Work Must Evolve Beyond Borders
Over the past few months, I’ve been actively applying for remote roles across global platforms. I’ve noticed a consistent pattern — one that many professionals may relate to, but few openly discuss.
Despite having the required technical skills, experience, and proven delivery, many applications don’t even reach the evaluation stage. The reason isn’t capability — it’s location, nationality, or visa constraints.
This raises an important question:
👉 In a world where work is done entirely on laptops, across distributed teams, why does geography still decide opportunity?
We collaborate across time zones.
We deploy systems to global users.
We solve problems asynchronously.
Yet, hiring decisions are often limited by borders rather than skills.
I understand there are valid business, legal, and compliance considerations. But at the same time, the remote work revolution has already shown us something powerful:
Talent is global. Opportunity should be too.
Imagine a hiring ecosystem where:
Skill validation matters more than passport origin
Compensation aligns with value delivered, not geography
Remote roles are truly location-agnostic, not “remote within specific countries”
For many professionals like myself, one checkbox — “Do you require visa sponsorship?” — becomes the silent filter that ends the journey before it even begins.
This isn’t frustration. It’s reflection.
Because the same system also reveals an opportunity:
🚀 The future of work is moving toward borderless execution models
🚀 Companies that embrace global talent without bias will win faster
🚀 Professionals must adapt by targeting truly remote-first ecosystems
I’m personally exploring:
Companies with global payroll models (EOR/contract-based hiring)
Remote-first startups that hire independent of geography
Building skills aligned with high-demand global tech (AI, MLOps, Cloud Architecture)
At the same time, I’ve come across multiple platforms promising “exclusive remote jobs” with high subscription fees. I’m still evaluating whether they truly add value or just repackage publicly available opportunities.
If you’ve navigated this challenge successfully — especially securing truly global remote roles without visa barriers — I’d genuinely value your insights.
Let’s open this conversation:
💬 Should skills outweigh geography in remote hiring?
💬 Are we moving toward a truly borderless workforce — or still far away?
💬 What platforms or strategies actually work?

We are Hiring!

Full-Stack Engineer (Backend-Heavy)
Experience: 2–4 years | Location: Kolkata / Hyderabad | Full-time

Who We Are
E-Solve Solutions is a nearly two-decade-old debt management company serving major banks, NBFCs, and telecom providers across India. We’re now building a technology arm: a digital lending platform (RBI Digital Lending Guidelines) and an AI agent-powered collections system. Real clients, real revenue, adding a serious tech layer.

Why This Role
Both products are built AI-native. Claude Code sessions, handoff documents as specs, adapter-pattern architecture. You’ll build with AI tools the way a previous generation built with Stack Overflow and libraries, except the tooling is more powerful and the workflow is faster. You’re still the engineer. You just ship more. And you’re expected to understand every function, every edge case in the codebase, whether you wrote it or an AI tool generated it.
The team is small by design. The AI-native workflow means 5 people cover ground that traditionally needs 15. The product is yours as much as anyone’s: read the PRD, read between the lines, ask clarifying questions, add features to the backlog, help guide the roadmap. Engineering judgment matters more than output volume here.

What You’ll Do
Own the backend across both products: APIs, database design, vendor integrations (KYC, credit bureaus, eSign, payment mandates, disbursals). Everything behind swappable adapter interfaces. Review others’ code as a real quality gate. No separate QA, engineers red-team each other, backed by automated tests and CI checks.

What We’re Looking For
Conceptual understanding matters more than specific framework experience. You know why you’d pick a relational DB over a document store. You can reason about what happens when two requests hit the same row. You think about API design as a contract, not just an endpoint. We use Node.js/TypeScript, Fastify, Prisma, PostgreSQL & Java.
You like puzzles. When a spec says “process the payment” and doesn’t define the failure path, you enjoy thinking through the five ways it could go wrong. You poke holes in requirements docs for fun.

Good to Have
Indian fintech APIs (Aadhaar, NACH, UPI, bureaus). RBI compliance familiarity. AWS experience. Agentic development with Claude Code, Cursor, or Copilot.

What This Is Not
This is not a role with a detailed Jira backlog and a PM defining every edge case. You set your own priorities within well-structured specs. Regulatory decisions come from the technical lead; you implement, you don’t interpret RBI circulars.

Competitive compensation. If you’re interested, apply for the given job role: https://lnkd.in/g4b9cj9S
